concepts.name: Tokenization
concepts.description: Splitting text into terms: lowercasing, stemming, stop words, n-grams.
concepts.mentalModel: Tokenization decides what counts as a 'word' for search. The same analyzer must run at index time and query time, or the query term will never match the indexed term.
commonMistakes: Using different analyzers for indexing and querying
Stemming too aggressively so distinct words collide
Dropping stop words that carry meaning in short queries
concepts.realWorldUsage: Elasticsearch/Lucene analyzers, Tantivy tokenizers, every lexical search engine.

concepts.name: Inverted Index
concepts.description: Term → posting list mapping that powers fast keyword lookup.
concepts.mentalModel: An inverted index flips documents inside out: instead of doc → terms, you store term → list of docs (postings). Query answering becomes intersecting/merging sorted posting lists.
commonMistakes: Storing postings unsorted, making intersection O(n*m)
Forgetting document frequency, which ranking later needs
Not separating the dictionary from the postings on disk
concepts.realWorldUsage: Lucene, Tantivy, PostgreSQL GIN indexes, every full-text search system.

resources.title: Stanford IR Book — The term vocabulary & postings lists

concepts.name: TF-IDF
concepts.description: Term frequency × inverse document frequency weighting for relevance.
concepts.mentalModel: TF-IDF says a term matters more when it appears often in a document (TF) but is rare across the corpus (IDF). It is the conceptual ancestor of BM25.
commonMistakes: Ignoring document length so long docs always win
Using raw counts instead of dampened term frequency
concepts.realWorldUsage: Baseline ranking, feature input to learning-to-rank models.

resources.title: Stanford IR Book — Scoring & term weighting

concepts.name: BM25
concepts.description: The standard lexical ranking function: TF saturation + IDF + length normalization.
concepts.mentalModel: BM25 fixes TF-IDF's two flaws: term frequency saturates (the 10th occurrence adds little via the k1 parameter) and document length is normalized (the b parameter). It is still the strongest single keyword ranker and the lexical half of hybrid search.
commonMistakes: Believing embeddings make BM25 obsolete — they are complementary
Leaving k1/b at defaults without tuning on your corpus
Skipping document length normalization
Not building search evals to compare against vector retrieval
concepts.realWorldUsage: Default scorer in Elasticsearch, OpenSearch, Lucene, Tantivy; lexical leg of hybrid retrieval.

concepts.name: Ranking Metrics
concepts.description: Precision, recall, MRR, and nDCG for measuring retrieval quality.
concepts.mentalModel: Precision/recall measure set quality; MRR rewards getting one right answer near the top; nDCG rewards graded relevance discounted by rank. Pick the metric that matches what users feel.
commonMistakes: Reporting accuracy instead of rank-aware metrics
Using nDCG without graded relevance labels
Measuring recall without a fixed cutoff k
concepts.realWorldUsage: Every search/recommendation quality dashboard.

resources.title: Stanford IR Book — Evaluation in IR

concepts.name: Search Evals
concepts.description: Building a labelled query set and harness to compare retrieval approaches.
concepts.mentalModel: You cannot improve retrieval you cannot measure. A search eval is a fixed set of queries with known-good results plus a harness that scores any retriever — it turns ranking changes from vibes into numbers.
commonMistakes: Tuning ranking without a frozen eval set
Letting the eval set leak into tuning decisions
Too few queries to detect real regressions
concepts.realWorldUsage: BEIR benchmark, internal relevance suites at every search company.

concepts.name: Hybrid Search
concepts.description: Fusing lexical (BM25) and vector retrieval, usually via reciprocal rank fusion.
concepts.mentalModel: Lexical search nails exact terms and rare tokens; vector search captures meaning. Hybrid search runs both and fuses the rankings (commonly Reciprocal Rank Fusion) so you get precision and recall together.
commonMistakes: Naively averaging incomparable BM25 and cosine scores
Skipping RRF and instead picking one retriever per query
Not re-evaluating after adding the second retriever
concepts.realWorldUsage: Production RAG retrieval, Elasticsearch hybrid, Weaviate/Qdrant hybrid modes.

concepts.name: Reranking
concepts.description: A second-stage cross-encoder pass that reorders the top-k candidates.
concepts.mentalModel: Retrieval is recall-oriented and cheap; reranking is precision-oriented and expensive. Retrieve ~100 candidates fast, then run a heavier cross-encoder on just those to reorder the top 10.
commonMistakes: Reranking the whole corpus instead of a candidate set
Adding a reranker without measuring latency budget
Not checking that reranking actually beats the retriever on evals
concepts.realWorldUsage: Cohere Rerank, cross-encoder rerankers in RAG pipelines.

concepts.name: Query Rewriting
concepts.description: Expanding, correcting, and normalizing queries before retrieval.

concepts.name: Search Systems
concepts.description: Inverted index, ranking, autocomplete at scale.

concepts.name: Embeddings
concepts.description: Mapping text/images into dense vectors where distance encodes meaning.
concepts.mentalModel: An embedding model projects content into a space where 'close' means 'semantically similar'. Search becomes geometry: find the nearest vectors to the query vector.
commonMistakes: Comparing vectors from two different embedding models
Forgetting to normalize when using cosine similarity
Ignoring the model's max input length and silently truncating
concepts.realWorldUsage: OpenAI/Cohere/Voyage embedding APIs, semantic search, RAG retrieval.

concepts.name: Vector Similarity
concepts.description: Cosine, dot product, and L2 distance — how to score vector closeness.
concepts.mentalModel: Cosine measures angle (direction), dot product measures angle and magnitude, L2 measures straight-line distance. On normalized vectors cosine and dot product rank identically, and L2 becomes a monotonic function of them.
commonMistakes: Mixing distance metrics between indexing and querying
Using dot product on un-normalized vectors and being surprised by magnitude bias
Assuming the metric does not matter for the chosen index
concepts.realWorldUsage: Every vector index exposes a metric choice; it must match the embedding model.

concepts.name: Top-k Vector Search
concepts.description: Returning the k nearest vectors to a query, exact or approximate.
concepts.mentalModel: Top-k is a bounded selection problem: maintain a size-k min-heap of best candidates while scanning. Exact search scans everything; ANN scans a clever subset.
commonMistakes: Sorting all scores instead of using a k-sized heap
Confusing recall@k (quality) with k (result count)

concepts.name: Brute-Force Vector DB
concepts.description: Exact nearest-neighbour search by scanning every vector — the correctness baseline.
concepts.mentalModel: Brute force computes the metric against every stored vector. It is O(n·d) and slow at scale, but it is exact — making it the recall=1.0 baseline that every ANN index is measured against.
commonMistakes: Skipping the brute-force baseline, so ANN recall is unmeasurable
Not batching the matrix multiply for the scan
Assuming brute force is always too slow — it is fine for <100k vectors
concepts.realWorldUsage: FAISS IndexFlat, the ground-truth oracle for ANN benchmarks.

concepts.name: HNSW
concepts.description: Hierarchical Navigable Small World graphs — the dominant ANN index.
concepts.mentalModel: HNSW builds a layered proximity graph: sparse upper layers for long hops, dense lower layers for fine search. A query greedily walks toward the nearest neighbour, descending layers — turning O(n) scan into O(log n) graph traversal.
commonMistakes: Tuning efSearch/efConstruction blindly without a recall curve
Ignoring that HNSW is memory-hungry and hard to update/delete
Expecting exact results — it is approximate by design
concepts.realWorldUsage: Default index in Qdrant, Weaviate, pgvector, Lucene/Elasticsearch kNN.

resources.title: Efficient and robust ANN using HNSW graphs (Malkov & Yashunin)

concepts.name: IVF (Inverted File)
concepts.description: Cluster vectors with k-means, then search only the nearest cells (nprobe).
concepts.mentalModel: IVF partitions the space into Voronoi cells via k-means. A query is matched to its nearest cells (nprobe of them) and only those vectors are scanned — trading recall for a big speedup.
commonMistakes: Setting nprobe=1 and wondering why recall is poor
Too few or too many centroids for the dataset size
concepts.realWorldUsage: FAISS IVF indexes, often combined with product quantization (IVF-PQ).

concepts.name: Product Quantization
concepts.description: Compress vectors into sub-space codebooks for tiny memory footprint.
concepts.mentalModel: PQ splits each vector into m sub-vectors and replaces each with the id of its nearest centroid in a per-sub-space codebook. A 1536-d float32 vector (6KB) shrinks to a handful of bytes, at the cost of approximate distances.
commonMistakes: Using PQ without IVF and losing the speedup
Too-aggressive compression that destroys recall
concepts.realWorldUsage: FAISS IVF-PQ, billion-scale vector search under a memory budget.

resources.title: Product Quantization for Nearest Neighbor Search (Jégou et al.)

concepts.name: Metadata Filtering
concepts.description: Combining vector search with structured predicates (tenant, date, tags).
concepts.mentalModel: Real queries are 'nearest vectors WHERE tenant=X AND date>Y'. Pre-filtering shrinks the candidate set before ANN (accurate, can be slow); post-filtering runs ANN then drops misses (fast, can return too few). The hard part is doing it inside a graph index.
commonMistakes: Post-filtering an HNSW result and returning fewer than k items
Treating filters as an afterthought to the index design
Ignoring multi-tenancy isolation in a shared index
concepts.realWorldUsage: Every production vector DB; critical for multi-tenant RAG.

concepts.name: Recall / Latency Tradeoffs
concepts.description: Reading recall-vs-latency curves to choose ANN parameters.
concepts.mentalModel: Every ANN knob trades recall for speed/memory. The honest artifact is a curve: recall on the y-axis, latency (or QPS, or memory) on the x-axis, with brute force as the recall=1.0 anchor.
commonMistakes: Reporting a single recall number with no latency context
Comparing indexes at different recall operating points
concepts.realWorldUsage: ann-benchmarks.com, every vector DB selection decision.

concepts.name: RAG
concepts.description: Retrieval-Augmented Generation: ground an LLM answer in retrieved context.
concepts.mentalModel: RAG = retrieve relevant chunks, pack them into the prompt, then generate. The quality ceiling is set by retrieval — a perfect LLM cannot answer from context it never received. Most 'RAG is bad' problems are retrieval problems.
commonMistakes: Blaming the LLM when retrieval failed
No eval set, so retrieval changes are guesswork
Stuffing too many chunks and burying the answer
Skipping reranking and hybrid retrieval
concepts.realWorldUsage: Doc Q&A, support bots, code assistants, HighSignal's intelligence engine.

concepts.name: Chunking
concepts.description: Splitting documents into retrievable units that preserve meaning.
concepts.mentalModel: A chunk is the atomic unit of retrieval — too big and embeddings blur, too small and context is lost. Good chunking respects structure (headings, functions, paragraphs) and adds overlap so answers are not cut in half.
commonMistakes: Fixed character splits that cut sentences mid-thought
Zero overlap, so boundary-spanning answers are lost
One chunk strategy for every document type
concepts.realWorldUsage: Every RAG ingestion pipeline.

concepts.name: Context Packing
concepts.description: Ordering and budgeting retrieved context within the model's window.
concepts.mentalModel: Context is a budget. Models attend unevenly (lost-in-the-middle), so order matters: put the strongest evidence at the edges, dedupe, and cut before you overflow.
commonMistakes: Filling the whole window because it is available
Ignoring position effects on attention

concepts.name: Structured Outputs
concepts.description: Forcing LLM output into a validated JSON schema.
concepts.mentalModel: Free text is unparseable glue between systems. Structured outputs constrain generation to a schema (via grammar/JSON mode) so the model's answer is a typed object your code can trust — or reject.
commonMistakes: Parsing JSON out of prose with regex instead of schema mode
No validation step, so malformed output crashes downstream
Over-nesting schemas the model cannot reliably fill
concepts.realWorldUsage: Function arguments, extraction pipelines, agent state, every LLM-to-code boundary.

concepts.name: Tool Calling
concepts.description: Letting an LLM invoke functions/APIs via structured calls.
concepts.mentalModel: Tool calling turns the LLM into a planner: it emits a structured request to a named tool, your code executes it, and the result goes back into context. The model never runs code — it decides what to run.
commonMistakes: Vague tool descriptions, so the model picks the wrong tool
No error handling for failed tool calls
Letting tools take dangerous actions without approval
concepts.realWorldUsage: MCP servers, agent frameworks, Claude/OpenAI tool use.

concepts.name: Agent Loops
concepts.description: The plan → act → observe loop, with memory and stopping conditions.
concepts.mentalModel: An agent is a loop: the model proposes an action, a tool executes it, the observation re-enters context, repeat until done. The engineering is in the guardrails — step limits, memory, and human approval — not the prompt.
commonMistakes: No step limit, so the loop runs away
Letting context grow unbounded across iterations
No human approval gate for irreversible actions
concepts.realWorldUsage: Coding agents, research agents, Aliveville's agent world.

concepts.name: Model Routing
concepts.description: Sending each request to the cheapest model that can handle it.
concepts.mentalModel: Not every request needs the frontier model. A router classifies difficulty and dispatches: small/cheap model for easy calls, large model for hard ones — with cost and latency tracked per route.
commonMistakes: Routing without evals, so quality silently drops
No fallback when the cheap model fails
concepts.realWorldUsage: Cost optimization in production LLM apps, the free-AI-first principle.

concepts.name: Prompt & Version Logging
concepts.description: Treating prompts as versioned artifacts with logged inputs/outputs.

concepts.name: LLM Evals
concepts.description: Measuring LLM output quality with datasets, graders, and LLM-as-judge.
concepts.mentalModel: Evals are unit tests for non-deterministic systems. Fix a dataset of inputs with expected behavior, define a grader (exact match, rubric, or LLM-as-judge), and run it on every prompt/model change.
commonMistakes: Shipping prompt changes on vibes with no eval
LLM-as-judge without checking the judge against humans
Eval set that does not cover real failure modes
concepts.realWorldUsage: Every serious LLM product; regression gates in CI.

resources.title: Your AI product needs evals (Hamel Husain)

concepts.name: ML Math Foundations
concepts.description: Vectors, matrices, dot products, matmul, shapes.

concepts.name: Gradient Descent
concepts.description: Loss surfaces, learning rate, SGD steps.

concepts.name: Softmax & Cross-Entropy
concepts.description: Logits to probabilities, negative log-likelihood loss.

concepts.name: Multi-Head Attention
concepts.description: Parallel heads, head_dim split, output projection.

concepts.name: Training & Debugging
concepts.description: Init, NaNs, overfit tests, gradient checks.

concepts.name: Checkpointing
concepts.description: Weights + optimizer state, resume, dataset manifests.

concepts.name: Style Data Engineering
concepts.description: Cleaning, dedup, JSONL tasks, memorization tests.

concepts.name: WebGPU Compute
concepts.description: WGSL, compute kernels, matmul, CPU parity.

concepts.name: HTTP Lifecycle
concepts.description: DNS → TCP/TLS → request → response: status codes, headers, keep-alive.
concepts.mentalModel: An HTTP request is a journey: resolve DNS, open a TCP connection, negotiate TLS, send the request, the server routes and responds, the connection is reused or closed. Latency and failures hide in each hop.
commonMistakes: Confusing 401 (unauthenticated) with 403 (unauthorized)
Ignoring connection reuse and paying TLS cost per request
Treating all 5xx as retryable and all 4xx as not
concepts.realWorldUsage: Every web service; the substrate under API design.

concepts.name: API Keys
concepts.description: Issuing, hashing, scoping, and rotating keys for machine clients.
concepts.mentalModel: An API key identifies a machine caller. Store only a hash (like a password), show the secret once, attach scopes, and support rotation — because a leaked key is inevitable.
commonMistakes: Storing API keys in plaintext
No prefix, so leaked keys are unsearchable in logs
No rotation or revocation path
concepts.realWorldUsage: Stripe, OpenAI, every developer platform.

concepts.name: Rate Limiting
concepts.description: Token bucket, leaky bucket, sliding window.
concepts.mentalModel: Rate limiting protects a shared resource from any one caller. Token bucket is the workhorse: tokens refill at a steady rate, each request spends one, an empty bucket means 429 — allowing bursts up to the bucket size.
commonMistakes: Fixed-window counters that allow 2x burst at the boundary
Limiting per-server instead of globally in a cluster
No Retry-After header on 429 responses
concepts.realWorldUsage: API gateways, every public API.

concepts.name: Idempotency
concepts.description: Idempotency keys and dedup windows for safe retries of mutations.
concepts.mentalModel: Networks make retries inevitable, and retrying a POST can charge a card twice. An idempotency key lets the client say 'this is the same operation' — the server stores the first result and replays it for duplicates.
commonMistakes: Assuming GET-only idempotency and leaving POST unsafe
No expiry on stored idempotency keys
Race between two concurrent requests with the same key
concepts.realWorldUsage: Stripe payments, any mutating API that clients retry.

resources.title: Stripe — Designing robust APIs with idempotency

concepts.name: Retries & DLQ
concepts.description: Backoff + jitter, poison messages, dead-letter queues.
concepts.mentalModel: Retries fix transient failures; backoff with jitter stops a retry storm; a dead-letter queue catches messages that will never succeed so they stop blocking the queue and become debuggable.
commonMistakes: Retrying without backoff and creating a thundering herd
Retrying without jitter so all clients sync up
No DLQ, so one poison message stalls the pipeline
Retrying non-idempotent work and causing double effects
concepts.realWorldUsage: SQS/Kafka consumers, every background job system.

concepts.name: Webhooks
concepts.description: Outbound event delivery: signing, retries, idempotent receivers.

concepts.name: Background Jobs
concepts.description: Offloading slow work to workers: scheduling, concurrency, visibility.

concepts.name: Caching
concepts.mentalModel: A cache trades memory and staleness for speed. The hard parts are not the hit — they are invalidation, the stampede when a hot key expires, and deciding how stale is acceptable.
commonMistakes: Cache-aside with no stampede protection on a hot key
No TTL, so stale data lives forever
Caching per-user data in a shared key
concepts.realWorldUsage: Redis/Memcached, CDN edges, HTTP caching.

concepts.name: Message Queues
concepts.mentalModel: A queue decouples producer speed from consumer speed and turns a sync call into a durable, retryable message. 'Exactly once' is mostly a myth — design for at-least-once delivery plus idempotent consumers.
commonMistakes: Assuming exactly-once delivery
Non-idempotent consumers on an at-least-once queue
Ignoring ordering guarantees (or their absence)
concepts.realWorldUsage: Kafka, SQS, RabbitMQ; ingestion and event pipelines.

concepts.name: Observability
concepts.mentalModel: The three pillars answer different questions: metrics say something is wrong, traces say where, logs say why. Structured logs and a request id stitch them together.
commonMistakes: Unstructured logs you cannot query
Alerting on causes instead of user-facing symptoms (SLOs)
No correlation id across services
concepts.realWorldUsage: OpenTelemetry, Datadog, PostHog, every production service.

concepts.name: API Design
concepts.mentalModel: An API is a contract you cannot easily change. Design for evolution: version it, paginate every list, make errors machine-readable, and keep resources consistent.
commonMistakes: Unpaginated list endpoints that fall over at scale
Breaking changes without a version bump
Inconsistent error shapes across endpoints
concepts.realWorldUsage: Every backend; the Codevetter and HighSignal public surfaces.

concepts.name: Auth Systems
concepts.mentalModel: Authentication proves who you are; authorization decides what you may do. JWTs are stateless and fast but hard to revoke; sessions are revocable but need a store. Pick per the revocation requirement.
commonMistakes: Confusing authentication with authorization
Long-lived JWTs with no revocation strategy
Trusting client-supplied roles
concepts.realWorldUsage: OAuth2/OIDC, every multi-user product.

concepts.name: B-Tree
concepts.description: The balanced, disk-friendly tree behind most relational indexes.
concepts.mentalModel: A B-tree is a wide, shallow tree sized to disk pages: each node holds many keys so the tree stays ~3-4 levels deep even for billions of rows. Reads are fast and in-place; writes do random page updates.
commonMistakes: Confusing B-tree (in-place, read-optimized) with LSM (append, write-optimized)
Ignoring page splits and write amplification
Forgetting that B-trees keep keys sorted, enabling range scans
concepts.realWorldUsage: PostgreSQL, MySQL/InnoDB, SQLite — the default index.

concepts.name: LSM Tree
concepts.description: Log-structured merge tree: write-optimized storage via sorted runs.
concepts.mentalModel: An LSM tree turns random writes into sequential ones: buffer writes in an in-memory memtable, flush to immutable sorted files (SSTables), and merge them in the background. Writes are fast; reads may touch several files (bloom filters help).
commonMistakes: Ignoring read amplification across SSTable levels
No bloom filters, so every read scans every file
Forgetting that deletes are tombstones, not removals
concepts.realWorldUsage: RocksDB, Cassandra, LevelDB, ScyllaDB; the write-heavy storage engine.

concepts.name: Write-Ahead Log
concepts.description: Append-only durability log written before the data pages.
concepts.mentalModel: The WAL is the database's promise: write the change to an append-only log and fsync it before touching the real data structure. A crash mid-write is recoverable by replaying the log. Durability before convenience.
commonMistakes: Acknowledging a write before the WAL is fsync'd
Letting the WAL grow forever with no checkpoint/truncation
Assuming a write() syscall means data is on disk
concepts.realWorldUsage: PostgreSQL WAL, SQLite WAL mode, every durable store and LSM engine.

concepts.name: Compaction
concepts.description: Merging sorted runs to reclaim space and bound read amplification.
concepts.mentalModel: An LSM tree keeps producing sorted files; without compaction reads get slower and deleted data lingers. Compaction merges files, drops tombstones, and bounds read amplification — at the cost of write amplification and IO spikes.
commonMistakes: Ignoring the write-amplification cost of compaction
Not understanding leveled vs size-tiered strategies
Compaction storms that starve foreground traffic
concepts.realWorldUsage: RocksDB/Cassandra compaction strategies; segment merges in search engines.

concepts.name: Object Storage
concepts.description: S3-style blob storage: cheap, durable, high-latency, immutable objects.
concepts.mentalModel: Object storage (S3/R2) is infinite, cheap, durable bytes — but with ~tens-of-ms latency and no in-place edits. Modern systems like Turbopuffer put cold data here and keep a hot cache in front. Storage and compute separate.
commonMistakes: Treating object storage like a low-latency disk
Many tiny objects instead of batched segment files
Ignoring request cost (GETs/PUTs are billed)
concepts.realWorldUsage: S3, Cloudflare R2, GCS; the durable layer of object-storage-first databases.

concepts.name: Columnar Storage
concepts.description: Column-oriented layout for analytics: compression and vectorized scans.

concepts.name: Secondary Indexes
concepts.description: Extra indexes for non-primary-key lookups, and what they cost on writes.

concepts.name: Search Platform Design
concepts.description: End-to-end design of a search platform: ingestion, indexing, query, ranking.
concepts.mentalModel: A search platform splits into an ingestion/indexing path (durable, batchy, write-optimized) and a query path (low-latency, cacheable, read-optimized). Segment files, an object-storage tier, and an eval harness tie them together.
commonMistakes: Designing the query path before the indexing path
No segment/compaction story for updates
Forgetting evals as part of the architecture

concepts.name: RAG System Design
concepts.description: Architecting a production RAG system: ingestion, retrieval, generation, evals.
concepts.mentalModel: RAG system design is two pipelines and a contract: an offline ingestion pipeline (chunk → embed → index) and an online query pipeline (retrieve → rerank → pack → generate), bound by an eval harness that gates every change.
commonMistakes: No offline/online separation
No eval gate, so quality regresses silently
Ignoring freshness and re-indexing

concepts.name: Binary Search
concepts.description: Halving sorted search space, predicate search.

concepts.name: Positioning
concepts.description: Naming the problem, the audience, and the alternative you beat.

concepts.name: Landing Pages
concepts.description: Turning a visitor into an activated user: hero, proof, single CTA.

concepts.name: SEO
concepts.description: Programmatic and content SEO: intent, structure, indexability.

concepts.name: Product Analytics
concepts.description: Activation, retention, funnels — measuring whether the product works.
